title: Midwifery nurses
description: Diagnose and coordinate all aspects of the birthing process, either independently or as part of a healthcare team. May provide well-woman gynecological care. Must have specialized, graduate nursing education. Apply knowledge of healthcare and information systems to assist in the design, development, and continued modification and analysis of computerized healthcare systems. Abstract, collect, and analyze treatment and followup information of patients. May educate staff and assist in problem solving to promote the implementation of the healthcare information system. May design, develop, test, and implement databases with complete history, diagnosis, treatment, and health status to help monitor diseases. Assist in operations, under the supervision of surgeons. May, in accordance with state laws, help surgeons to make incisions and close surgical sites, manipulate or remove tissues, implant surgical devices or drains, suction the surgical site, place catheters, clamp or cauterize vessels or tissue, and apply dressings to surgical site. (Based on 5 related US occupations.)

tasks.task: Conduct clinical research on topics such as maternal or infant health care, contraceptive methods, breastfeeding, and gynecological care.
Develop, implement, or evaluate individualized plans for midwifery care and health care management.
Read current literature, talk with colleagues, or participate in professional organizations to keep abreast of developments in midwifery.
Educate patients and family members regarding prenatal, intrapartum, postpartum, newborn, or interconception care, including family planning services.
Provide prenatal, intrapartum, postpartum, and newborn care to patients, managing newborn care during the first weeks of life.
Document patients' health histories, symptoms, physical conditions, and findings of physical examinations, including vital signs and diagnostic information.
Monitor fetal development by listening to fetal heartbeat, taking external uterine measurements, and estimating fetal size and weight.
Perform physical examinations, including pelvic exams and neurological reflex checks, to monitor maternal and fetal health.
Consult with or refer patients to appropriate specialists when conditions exceed the scope of practice or expertise, including for ultrasound and genetic testing.
Establish practice guidelines for specialty areas such as primary health care of women, care of the childbearing family, and newborn care.
Initiate emergency interventions to stabilize patients and perform postpartum health assessments of mothers and babies at regular intervals.
Provide information about community health and social resources, and counsel women regarding the nutritional requirements of pregnancy.
Monitor maternal condition during labor by checking vital signs and uterine contractions, and assist maternal patients in finding positions to facilitate childbirth.
Evaluate patients' laboratory and medical records, and perform ongoing prenatal health assessments to track changes in physical and emotional health.
Provide, or refer patients to other providers for, education or counseling on topics such as genetic testing, newborn care, contraception, or breastfeeding.
Perform post-partum health assessments of mothers and babies at regular intervals and assist with patient comfort measures during labor.
Establish and follow emergency or contingency plans for mothers and newborns, and monitor maternal and fetal well-being throughout the birthing process.
Provide necessary medical care for infants at birth, including emergency care such as resuscitation.
